L-Glutamine Supplementation in COVID-19 Clinical
Outcomes
what's the l-glutamine supplementation impact on morbidity and mortality of COVID patients ?
Exploratory — 2026-04-10
Abstract
Background: L-glutamine is a conditionally essential amino acid that is frequently depleted during the
extreme physiological stress of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection.
This depletion exacerbates immune and endothelial dysfunction. Despite mechanistic rationales for
exogenous supplementation, its clinical impact on coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) morbidity and
mortality remains equivocal. Methods: We conducted an exploratory scoping review adhering to the
Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews
(PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. We synthesized clinical observational data, randomized controlled trials, and
quasi-targeted metabolomic profiles to evaluate the efficacy of L-glutamine supplementation in COVID-19
patients. Results: Preliminary clinical data indicate that early oral L-glutamine supplementation
significantly reduces hospitalization duration and intensive care unit (ICU) admission rates in non-critical
patients. In critically ill ICU cohorts, intravenous supplementation added to enteral nutrition significantly
decreased the need for total parenteral nutrition. However, neither intervention demonstrated a statistically
significant reduction in short-term mortality. A profound metabolomic paradox emerges: while host cells
require glutamine for immune homeostasis, SARS-CoV-2 exploits the same glutamine metabolic pathways
for viral replication. Conclusions: L-glutamine supplementation serves as a promising adjunct therapy to
reduce COVID-19 morbidity, particularly when administered early. However, its ultimate efficacy is highly
dependent on baseline metabolic status and disease timing. Future large-scale, baseline-stratified trials
are necessary to optimize administration protocols and mitigate potential viral replication risks.
*
1. Topic Landscape: L-Glutamine in Immune and Endothelial
Function
L-glutamine is the most abundant non-essential amino acid in the human body, constituting approximately
30% of the circulating plasma free amino acid pool [15]. During periods of extreme physiological stress,
catabolic states, or severe viral infections, endogenous production frequently fails to meet metabolic
demands, rendering L-glutamine a "conditionally essential" nutrient [2]. L-glutamine plays a critical role in
cellular homeostasis, acting as a primary oxidative fuel for rapidly dividing cells, including lymphocytes,
enterocytes, and macrophages [1]. Furthermore, adequate glutamine levels are vital for preserving
endothelial cellular integrity and facilitating robust, coordinated immune responses [3]. The attenuation of
inflammatory cascades and the maintenance of vascular health rely heavily on optimal glutamine
availability, highlighting its therapeutic potential in severe infectious diseases [1].
The pathogenesis of COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2, is heavily driven by aberrant systemic
inflammation and immune hyperactivation [9][12]. In severe clinical presentations, this hyperinflammatory
state frequently precipitates acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), systemic tissue hypoxemia, and
widespread endothelial damage [6][12], inducing profound metabolic dysregulation [13]. Advanced

1. Topic Landscape: L-Glutamine in Immune and Endothelial
Function
L-glutamine is the most abundant non-essential amino acid in the human body, constituting approximately

enterocytes, and macrophages [1]. Furthermore, adequate glutamine levels are vital for preserving
endothelial cellular integrity and facilitating robust, coordinated immune responses [3]. The attenuation of
inflammatory cascades and the maintenance of vascular health rely heavily on optimal glutamine
availability, highlighting its therapeutic potential in severe infectious diseases [1].
The pathogenesis of COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2, is heavily driven by aberrant systemic
inflammation and immune hyperactivation [9][12]. In severe clinical presentations, this hyperinflammatory
state frequently precipitates acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), systemic tissue hypoxemia, and
widespread endothelial damage [6][12], inducing profound metabolic dysregulation [13]. Advanced
metabolomic and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic profiling of COVID-19 patients
consistently reveals marked alterations in the blood serum metabolome, notably characterized by
significant deviations in plasma amino acid concentrations [4][7][19]. Chief among these metabolic
anomalies is a severe depletion of circulating glutamine and a marked dysregulation of the

pages.text:
glutamine/glutamate metabolic axis [8]. Because the catabolic rate during acute SARS-CoV-2 infection
drastically outpaces endogenous synthesis, the resulting glutamine deficiency actively promotes further
immune and endothelial cell dysfunction, exacerbating disease severity [3].
2. Key Questions and Sub-Questions Identified
The convergence of systemic glutamine depletion and severe COVID-19 symptomatology poses several
critical clinical questions regarding therapeutic intervention. Primarily, what is the direct impact of
exogenous L-glutamine supplementation on the morbidity and short-term mortality of patients admitted
with COVID-19?
Secondary sub-questions include: (i) Does the route of administration (enteral versus intravenous)
distinctly alter clinical outcomes such as ICU admission rates and hospitalization duration [1][15]? (ii) Can
L-glutamine supplementation effectively modulate and suppress the hyperinflammatory response
associated with SARS-CoV-2 [15]? (iii) How do we reconcile the immune-boosting benefits of glutamine
with recent evidence suggesting that coronaviruses may exploit host cellular glutamine metabolism to
facilitate their own viral replication [17]?
While early clinical trials suggest that L-glutamine may shorten hospital stays and reduce the need for
ICU-level care [1], its definitive role in preventing short-term mortality remains ambiguous and requires
deeper investigation [15].
Methods
Study Design and Guidelines Compliance To systematically address these research questions, this
exploratory investigation was structured in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic
reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines, an appropriate
framework for mapping emerging heterogeneous evidence [4][8]. By integrating clinical trial outcomes with
metabolomic profiling, this design allows for a comprehensive evaluation of how conditional amino acid
interventions alter the clinical trajectory of COVID-19 [2][11].
Ethical Considerations As this study constitutes a systematic scoping review and synthesis of previously
published and publicly available literature, institutional review board (IRB) ethical approval was waived.
Preliminary Literature Scan Methodology A comprehensive preliminary literature scan was conducted
to identify relevant clinical and metabolic studies. The search strategy utilized electronic databases,
primarily MEDLINE and the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, reflecting validated
methodologies for scoping reviews in clinical nutrition [2]. Search terms included combinations of
"COVID-19", "SARS-CoV-2", "L-glutamine", "glutamine supplementation", "metabolomics", "intensive
care", and "mortality".
Inclusion criteria for human studies required that articles report on adult patients diagnosed with COVID-19
who received oral or intravenous L-glutamine supplementation, or studies that analyzed
glutamine/glutamate metabolic dysregulation via NMR spectroscopy or quasi-targeted metabolomics
[4][8]. Exclusion criteria encompassed non-human in vivo models [18], purely in vitro analyses lacking
direct clinical translation [9], and studies where baseline nutritional status or supplementation parameters
were not quantifiable [2].
Risk of Bias Assessment To ensure data integrity, the risk of bias for included clinical trials was
assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2) for randomized trials and the Risk Of Bias In
Non-randomized Studies of Interventions (ROBINS-I) tool for observational cohorts. Studies exhibiting

Definition of Variables of Interest Primary and secondary variables of interest were explicitly defined:
(cid:127) L-glutamine Dosage and Route: The administration route (oral vs. intravenous) and dosage
protocols utilized during the acute phase of infection or ICU admission [1][15].
(cid:127) Hospitalization Time: Total duration (in days) from admission to discharge, utilized as a primary
metric for morbidity [1].
(cid:127) ICU Admission Rates: The proportion of patients requiring escalation to intensive care [1].
(cid:127) Mortality: Short-term mortality occurring within the observation period (e.g., 7-day ICU mortality or
total in-hospital mortality) [1][15].
Statistical and Thematic Analysis Plan Data synthesis employed a dual approach encompassing
thematic analysis of metabolomic data and statistical synthesis of clinical outcomes. Continuous variables
(e.g., hospitalization time) were analyzed by extracting mean durations, standard deviations, and reported
p-values to evaluate effect sizes [1]. Categorical variables (mortality, ICU admission rates) were evaluated
by comparing proportional differences between standard enteral nutrition (EN) cohorts and
glutamine-supplemented (GN) cohorts [15]. The statistical analysis plan ensured that all comparisons were
extracted symmetrically, with significance thresholds set at p < 0.05, and missing variables were explicitly
coded to prevent data truncation or skewed comparative weighting. Table 1 summarizes the planned
comparative data extraction framework.

3. Preliminary Literature Scan with Emerging Themes
The synthesized literature reveals a focused pool of clinical data evaluating L-glutamine supplementation
in COVID-19 patients, primarily anchored by a prospective observational study of early-stage patients [1]
and a randomized clinical trial of severe ICU patients [15].

intravenous glutamine added to enteral nutrition significantly reduced the rate of unsuccessful enteral
feeding; only 33.3% of supplemented patients required a transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN),
compared to 73.3% of controls (p=0.002) [15].
Mortality Outcomes Despite observable improvements in morbidity, evidence supporting L-glutamine's
efficacy in reducing mortality remains limited. In the ICU cohort, short-term (7-day) mortality was not
significantly altered by glutamine supplementation (33.3% vs. 37.6%) [15]. Similarly, in the earlier-stage
cohort, overall mortality was exceptionally low, preventing robust statistical conclusions regarding survival
[1].
4. Identified Knowledge Gaps
Despite promising signals regarding morbidity, fundamental gaps in the literature hinder the development
of standardized clinical guidelines. The scoping review by Rozga et al. highlights a critical deficiency: most
trials fail to measure baseline glutamine status prior to intervention, making it impossible to differentiate
between the correction of a deficiency and supra-therapeutic supplementation [2].
Key Knowledge Gaps include:
(cid:127) Optimal Timing and Dosing: Does early oral supplementation block viral progression, or does it
fuel viral replication [1][17]? Is late-stage IV intervention futile for mortality once hyperinflammation
has triggered irreversible tissue damage [15]?
(cid:127) Patient Stratification: How do patient-specific metabolic phenotypes—such as
hypertension-associated NMR metabolomic profiles [4] or cancer-induced immune cuproptosis
[14]—alter the efficacy of exogenous glutamine?
5. Potential Hypotheses for Further Investigation
Based on the exploratory synthesis and the identified gaps, the following testable hypotheses (speculative
insights) are proposed:
(cid:127) Hypothesis 1 (The Temporal Dependency Hypothesis): Early enteral L-glutamine
supplementation prevents progression to severe COVID-19 by preserving gut barrier integrity and
preventing dysbiosis, whereas late IV administration in the ICU primarily functions to mitigate systemic
hypercatabolism without altering viral-induced mortality.
(cid:127) Hypothesis 2 (The Phenotype Stratification Hypothesis): Patients with baseline endothelial
dysfunction (e.g., Sickle Cell Disease [16], Hypertension [4]) will exhibit a disproportionately greater
absolute risk reduction in COVID-19 morbidity when treated with L-glutamine compared to
normotensive, non-SCD patients.
(cid:127) Hypothesis 3 (The Viral Uncoupling Hypothesis): Viral hijacking of host glutamine metabolism
can be therapeutically uncoupled from host immune utilization through the co-administration of
targeted metabolic inhibitors alongside L-glutamine.
6. Suggested Methodologies for Each Hypothesis
To rigorously test these hypotheses, future investigations should employ the following methodologies:
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 1: Employ a longitudinal, multi-arm clinical cohort design administering L-glutamine
during early infection (viral replication phase) versus late infection (hyperinflammatory phase). Track
daily viral shedding (via RT-PCR) alongside continuous monitoring of inflammatory cytokines to
delineate the risk-benefit ratio at different disease stages [1][15].

pages.text:
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 2: Conduct prospective, multi-center, double-blind RCTs. Utilize quantitative NMR
spectroscopy and targeted metabolomics to establish baseline plasma glutamine/glutamate ratios
[4][8] before initiating supplementation. Stratify cohorts based on preexisting endothelial conditions
[4][16].
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 3: Perform in vitro metabolic tracing studies using stable isotope-labeled
L-glutamine in human airway epithelial cells. Pair glutamine supplementation with selective inhibitors
of viral nucleotide synthesis pathways, such as CTP synthetase 1 (CTPS1) inhibitors [5].
7. Cross-Disciplinary Connections
Integrating clinical findings with metabolomic profiling yields a profound cross-disciplinary landscape.
Quantitative NMR spectroscopy consistently identifies severe dysregulation of glutamine/glutamate
metabolism in COVID-19 patients, correlating strongly with clinical severity [4][8][11][19]. This provides a
mechanistic basis for clinical observations: L-glutamine replenishes depleted stores, dampening
inflammation [3].
However, a paradoxical speculative insight arises from virology: glutamine metabolism is fundamentally
essential for coronavirus replication within host cells [17]. This introduces a "metabolic tug-of-war."
Furthermore, insights from Oncology regarding immune cell cuproptosis and glutamine regulation [14] can
inform immunological endpoints in COVID-19 trials, while leveraging Hematology models of tissue
hypoxemia [6] can refine glutamine's use in mitigating COVID-19 vasculopathy, particularly in Sickle Cell
Disease [10][16].
8. Conclusion and Recommended Next Steps
This exploratory investigation highlights a complex but promising landscape regarding L-glutamine
administration in SARS-CoV-2 infection. Early evidence suggests L-glutamine serves as a beneficial
adjunct therapy, significantly shortening hospitalization time and reducing ICU admission in non-critical
patients [1], and mitigating the transition to total parenteral nutrition in critically ill patients [15]. However,
evidence supporting a direct reduction in short-term mortality remains inconclusive [1][15], complicated by
the viral reliance on host glutamine for replication [17].
To translate these exploratory findings into actionable clinical paradigms, the following next steps are
recommended, prioritized by urgency:
1. Priority 1 (Metabolomic-Stratified RCTs): Execute large-scale, double-blind RCTs that stratify
COVID-19 patients based on baseline plasma glutamine levels using NMR spectroscopy [11] to determine
if supplementation uniquely benefits those with proven deficiency [2].
2. Priority 2 (Viral Substrate Profiling): Initiate in vivo translational studies to map the competitive uptake
of labeled L-glutamine isotopes between host immune cells and SARS-CoV-2 replication machinery
[17][18].
3. Priority 3 (Special Population Protocols): Design targeted pilot studies for COVID-19 patients with
extra-pulmonary comorbidities, such as Sickle Cell Disease [6][10][16], leveraging existing L-glutamine
pharmacovigilance data to establish dual-benefit therapeutic protocols.
4. Priority 4 (Update Nutritional Guidelines): Policymakers should update interim guidelines to
recognize the potential of enteral L-glutamine in reducing hospital stay and TPN reliance [1][15], utilizing
digital health tools to monitor outpatient nutritional adherence during post-COVID recovery [13][20].

3. Priority 3 (Special Population Protocols): Design targeted pilot studies for COVID-19 patients with
extra-pulmonary comorbidities, such as Sickle Cell Disease [6][10][16], leveraging existing L-glutamine
pharmacovigilance data to establish dual-benefit therapeutic protocols.
4. Priority 4 (Update Nutritional Guidelines): Policymakers should update interim guidelines to
recognize the potential of enteral L-glutamine in reducing hospital stay and TPN reliance [1][15], utilizing
digital health tools to monitor outpatient nutritional adherence during post-COVID recovery [13][20].

full_text:
L-Glutamine Supplementation in COVID-19 Clinical
Outcomes
what's the l-glutamine supplementation impact on morbidity and mortality of COVID patients ?
Exploratory — 2026-04-10
Abstract
Background: L-glutamine is a conditionally essential amino acid that is frequently depleted during the
extreme physiological stress of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection.
This depletion exacerbates immune and endothelial dysfunction. Despite mechanistic rationales for
exogenous supplementation, its clinical impact on coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) morbidity and
mortality remains equivocal. Methods: We conducted an exploratory scoping review adhering to the
Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews
(PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. We synthesized clinical observational data, randomized controlled trials, and
quasi-targeted metabolomic profiles to evaluate the efficacy of L-glutamine supplementation in COVID-19
patients. Results: Preliminary clinical data indicate that early oral L-glutamine supplementation
significantly reduces hospitalization duration and intensive care unit (ICU) admission rates in non-critical
patients. In critically ill ICU cohorts, intravenous supplementation added to enteral nutrition significantly
decreased the need for total parenteral nutrition. However, neither intervention demonstrated a statistically
significant reduction in short-term mortality. A profound metabolomic paradox emerges: while host cells
require glutamine for immune homeostasis, SARS-CoV-2 exploits the same glutamine metabolic pathways
for viral replication. Conclusions: L-glutamine supplementation serves as a promising adjunct therapy to
reduce COVID-19 morbidity, particularly when administered early. However, its ultimate efficacy is highly
dependent on baseline metabolic status and disease timing. Future large-scale, baseline-stratified trials
are necessary to optimize administration protocols and mitigate potential viral replication risks.
*
1. Topic Landscape: L-Glutamine in Immune and Endothelial
Function
L-glutamine is the most abundant non-essential amino acid in the human body, constituting approximately
30% of the circulating plasma free amino acid pool [15]. During periods of extreme physiological stress,
catabolic states, or severe viral infections, endogenous production frequently fails to meet metabolic
demands, rendering L-glutamine a "conditionally essential" nutrient [2]. L-glutamine plays a critical role in
cellular homeostasis, acting as a primary oxidative fuel for rapidly dividing cells, including lymphocytes,
enterocytes, and macrophages [1]. Furthermore, adequate glutamine levels are vital for preserving
endothelial cellular integrity and facilitating robust, coordinated immune responses [3]. The attenuation of
inflammatory cascades and the maintenance of vascular health rely heavily on optimal glutamine
availability, highlighting its therapeutic potential in severe infectious diseases [1].
The pathogenesis of COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2, is heavily driven by aberrant systemic
inflammation and immune hyperactivation [9][12]. In severe clinical presentations, this hyperinflammatory
state frequently precipitates acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), systemic tissue hypoxemia, and
widespread endothelial damage [6][12], inducing profound metabolic dysregulation [13]. Advanced
metabolomic and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic profiling of COVID-19 patients
consistently reveals marked alterations in the blood serum metabolome, notably characterized by
significant deviations in plasma amino acid concentrations [4][7][19]. Chief among these metabolic
anomalies is a severe depletion of circulating glutamine and a marked dysregulation of the

glutamine/glutamate metabolic axis [8]. Because the catabolic rate during acute SARS-CoV-2 infection
drastically outpaces endogenous synthesis, the resulting glutamine deficiency actively promotes further
immune and endothelial cell dysfunction, exacerbating disease severity [3].
2. Key Questions and Sub-Questions Identified
The convergence of systemic glutamine depletion and severe COVID-19 symptomatology poses several
critical clinical questions regarding therapeutic intervention. Primarily, what is the direct impact of
exogenous L-glutamine supplementation on the morbidity and short-term mortality of patients admitted
with COVID-19?
Secondary sub-questions include: (i) Does the route of administration (enteral versus intravenous)
distinctly alter clinical outcomes such as ICU admission rates and hospitalization duration [1][15]? (ii) Can
L-glutamine supplementation effectively modulate and suppress the hyperinflammatory response
associated with SARS-CoV-2 [15]? (iii) How do we reconcile the immune-boosting benefits of glutamine
with recent evidence suggesting that coronaviruses may exploit host cellular glutamine metabolism to
facilitate their own viral replication [17]?
While early clinical trials suggest that L-glutamine may shorten hospital stays and reduce the need for
ICU-level care [1], its definitive role in preventing short-term mortality remains ambiguous and requires
deeper investigation [15].
Methods
Study Design and Guidelines Compliance To systematically address these research questions, this
exploratory investigation was structured in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic
reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines, an appropriate
framework for mapping emerging heterogeneous evidence [4][8]. By integrating clinical trial outcomes with
metabolomic profiling, this design allows for a comprehensive evaluation of how conditional amino acid
interventions alter the clinical trajectory of COVID-19 [2][11].
Ethical Considerations As this study constitutes a systematic scoping review and synthesis of previously
published and publicly available literature, institutional review board (IRB) ethical approval was waived.
Preliminary Literature Scan Methodology A comprehensive preliminary literature scan was conducted
to identify relevant clinical and metabolic studies. The search strategy utilized electronic databases,
primarily MEDLINE and the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, reflecting validated
methodologies for scoping reviews in clinical nutrition [2]. Search terms included combinations of
"COVID-19", "SARS-CoV-2", "L-glutamine", "glutamine supplementation", "metabolomics", "intensive
care", and "mortality".
Inclusion criteria for human studies required that articles report on adult patients diagnosed with COVID-19
who received oral or intravenous L-glutamine supplementation, or studies that analyzed
glutamine/glutamate metabolic dysregulation via NMR spectroscopy or quasi-targeted metabolomics
[4][8]. Exclusion criteria encompassed non-human in vivo models [18], purely in vitro analyses lacking
direct clinical translation [9], and studies where baseline nutritional status or supplementation parameters
were not quantifiable [2].
Risk of Bias Assessment To ensure data integrity, the risk of bias for included clinical trials was
assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2) for randomized trials and the Risk Of Bias In
Non-randomized Studies of Interventions (ROBINS-I) tool for observational cohorts. Studies exhibiting

critically high bias due to unmeasured confounding were noted during thematic synthesis.
Definition of Variables of Interest Primary and secondary variables of interest were explicitly defined:
(cid:127) L-glutamine Dosage and Route: The administration route (oral vs. intravenous) and dosage
protocols utilized during the acute phase of infection or ICU admission [1][15].
(cid:127) Hospitalization Time: Total duration (in days) from admission to discharge, utilized as a primary
metric for morbidity [1].
(cid:127) ICU Admission Rates: The proportion of patients requiring escalation to intensive care [1].
(cid:127) Mortality: Short-term mortality occurring within the observation period (e.g., 7-day ICU mortality or
total in-hospital mortality) [1][15].
Statistical and Thematic Analysis Plan Data synthesis employed a dual approach encompassing
thematic analysis of metabolomic data and statistical synthesis of clinical outcomes. Continuous variables
(e.g., hospitalization time) were analyzed by extracting mean durations, standard deviations, and reported
p-values to evaluate effect sizes [1]. Categorical variables (mortality, ICU admission rates) were evaluated
by comparing proportional differences between standard enteral nutrition (EN) cohorts and
glutamine-supplemented (GN) cohorts [15]. The statistical analysis plan ensured that all comparisons were
extracted symmetrically, with significance thresholds set at p < 0.05, and missing variables were explicitly
coded to prevent data truncation or skewed comparative weighting. Table 1 summarizes the planned
comparative data extraction framework.

Transitioning from our methodological framework, the synthesis of extracted data generated distinct
clinical and metabolomic themes regarding the efficacy of L-glutamine.
3. Preliminary Literature Scan with Emerging Themes
The synthesized literature reveals a focused pool of clinical data evaluating L-glutamine supplementation
in COVID-19 patients, primarily anchored by a prospective observational study of early-stage patients [1]
and a randomized clinical trial of severe ICU patients [15].
Impact on Morbidity: Hospitalization and ICU Requirements L-glutamine supplementation significantly
improves specific morbidity indicators when administered early. In a cohort of 60 early-stage patients,
those receiving oral L-glutamine exhibited a reduced mean hospitalization duration (8.9 ± 1.8 days)
compared to unsupplemented controls (10.4 ± 1.9 days) (p=0.005) [1]. Furthermore, oral supplementation
eliminated the need for ICU transfer in this cohort (0% vs. 13.3%, p=0.038) [1]. In critically ill populations,

intravenous glutamine added to enteral nutrition significantly reduced the rate of unsuccessful enteral
feeding; only 33.3% of supplemented patients required a transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN),
compared to 73.3% of controls (p=0.002) [15].
Mortality Outcomes Despite observable improvements in morbidity, evidence supporting L-glutamine's
efficacy in reducing mortality remains limited. In the ICU cohort, short-term (7-day) mortality was not
significantly altered by glutamine supplementation (33.3% vs. 37.6%) [15]. Similarly, in the earlier-stage
cohort, overall mortality was exceptionally low, preventing robust statistical conclusions regarding survival
[1].
4. Identified Knowledge Gaps
Despite promising signals regarding morbidity, fundamental gaps in the literature hinder the development
of standardized clinical guidelines. The scoping review by Rozga et al. highlights a critical deficiency: most
trials fail to measure baseline glutamine status prior to intervention, making it impossible to differentiate
between the correction of a deficiency and supra-therapeutic supplementation [2].
Key Knowledge Gaps include:
(cid:127) Optimal Timing and Dosing: Does early oral supplementation block viral progression, or does it
fuel viral replication [1][17]? Is late-stage IV intervention futile for mortality once hyperinflammation
has triggered irreversible tissue damage [15]?
(cid:127) Patient Stratification: How do patient-specific metabolic phenotypes—such as
hypertension-associated NMR metabolomic profiles [4] or cancer-induced immune cuproptosis
[14]—alter the efficacy of exogenous glutamine?
5. Potential Hypotheses for Further Investigation
Based on the exploratory synthesis and the identified gaps, the following testable hypotheses (speculative
insights) are proposed:
(cid:127) Hypothesis 1 (The Temporal Dependency Hypothesis): Early enteral L-glutamine
supplementation prevents progression to severe COVID-19 by preserving gut barrier integrity and
preventing dysbiosis, whereas late IV administration in the ICU primarily functions to mitigate systemic
hypercatabolism without altering viral-induced mortality.
(cid:127) Hypothesis 2 (The Phenotype Stratification Hypothesis): Patients with baseline endothelial
dysfunction (e.g., Sickle Cell Disease [16], Hypertension [4]) will exhibit a disproportionately greater
absolute risk reduction in COVID-19 morbidity when treated with L-glutamine compared to
normotensive, non-SCD patients.
(cid:127) Hypothesis 3 (The Viral Uncoupling Hypothesis): Viral hijacking of host glutamine metabolism
can be therapeutically uncoupled from host immune utilization through the co-administration of
targeted metabolic inhibitors alongside L-glutamine.
6. Suggested Methodologies for Each Hypothesis
To rigorously test these hypotheses, future investigations should employ the following methodologies:
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 1: Employ a longitudinal, multi-arm clinical cohort design administering L-glutamine
during early infection (viral replication phase) versus late infection (hyperinflammatory phase). Track
daily viral shedding (via RT-PCR) alongside continuous monitoring of inflammatory cytokines to
delineate the risk-benefit ratio at different disease stages [1][15].

(cid:127) For Hypothesis 2: Conduct prospective, multi-center, double-blind RCTs. Utilize quantitative NMR
spectroscopy and targeted metabolomics to establish baseline plasma glutamine/glutamate ratios
[4][8] before initiating supplementation. Stratify cohorts based on preexisting endothelial conditions
[4][16].
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 3: Perform in vitro metabolic tracing studies using stable isotope-labeled
L-glutamine in human airway epithelial cells. Pair glutamine supplementation with selective inhibitors
of viral nucleotide synthesis pathways, such as CTP synthetase 1 (CTPS1) inhibitors [5].
7. Cross-Disciplinary Connections
Integrating clinical findings with metabolomic profiling yields a profound cross-disciplinary landscape.
Quantitative NMR spectroscopy consistently identifies severe dysregulation of glutamine/glutamate
metabolism in COVID-19 patients, correlating strongly with clinical severity [4][8][11][19]. This provides a
mechanistic basis for clinical observations: L-glutamine replenishes depleted stores, dampening
inflammation [3].
However, a paradoxical speculative insight arises from virology: glutamine metabolism is fundamentally
essential for coronavirus replication within host cells [17]. This introduces a "metabolic tug-of-war."
Furthermore, insights from Oncology regarding immune cell cuproptosis and glutamine regulation [14] can
inform immunological endpoints in COVID-19 trials, while leveraging Hematology models of tissue
hypoxemia [6] can refine glutamine's use in mitigating COVID-19 vasculopathy, particularly in Sickle Cell
Disease [10][16].
8. Conclusion and Recommended Next Steps
This exploratory investigation highlights a complex but promising landscape regarding L-glutamine
administration in SARS-CoV-2 infection. Early evidence suggests L-glutamine serves as a beneficial
adjunct therapy, significantly shortening hospitalization time and reducing ICU admission in non-critical
patients [1], and mitigating the transition to total parenteral nutrition in critically ill patients [15]. However,
evidence supporting a direct reduction in short-term mortality remains inconclusive [1][15], complicated by
the viral reliance on host glutamine for replication [17].
To translate these exploratory findings into actionable clinical paradigms, the following next steps are
recommended, prioritized by urgency:
1. Priority 1 (Metabolomic-Stratified RCTs): Execute large-scale, double-blind RCTs that stratify
COVID-19 patients based on baseline plasma glutamine levels using NMR spectroscopy [11] to determine
if supplementation uniquely benefits those with proven deficiency [2].
2. Priority 2 (Viral Substrate Profiling): Initiate in vivo translational studies to map the competitive uptake
of labeled L-glutamine isotopes between host immune cells and SARS-CoV-2 replication machinery
[17][18].
3. Priority 3 (Special Population Protocols): Design targeted pilot studies for COVID-19 patients with
extra-pulmonary comorbidities, such as Sickle Cell Disease [6][10][16], leveraging existing L-glutamine
pharmacovigilance data to establish dual-benefit therapeutic protocols.
4. Priority 4 (Update Nutritional Guidelines): Policymakers should update interim guidelines to
recognize the potential of enteral L-glutamine in reducing hospital stay and TPN reliance [1][15], utilizing
digital health tools to monitor outpatient nutritional adherence during post-COVID recovery [13][20].

L-Glutamine Supplementation in COVID-19 Clinical
Outcomes
what's the l-glutamine supplementation impact on morbidity and mortality of COVID patients ?
Exploratory — 2026-04-10
Abstract
Background: L-glutamine is a conditionally essential amino acid that is frequently depleted during the
extreme physiological stress of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection.
This depletion exacerbates immune and endothelial dysfunction. Despite mechanistic rationales for
exogenous supplementation, its clinical impact on coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) morbidity and
mortality remains equivocal. Methods: We conducted an exploratory scoping review adhering to the
Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews
(PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. We synthesized clinical observational data, randomized controlled trials, and
quasi-targeted metabolomic profiles to evaluate the efficacy of L-glutamine supplementation in COVID-19
patients. Results: Preliminary clinical data indicate that early oral L-glutamine supplementation
significantly reduces hospitalization duration and intensive care unit (ICU) admission rates in non-critical
patients. In critically ill ICU cohorts, intravenous supplementation added to enteral nutrition significantly
decreased the need for total parenteral nutrition. However, neither intervention demonstrated a statistically
significant reduction in short-term mortality. A profound metabolomic paradox emerges: while host cells
require glutamine for immune homeostasis, SARS-CoV-2 exploits the same glutamine metabolic pathways
for viral replication. Conclusions: L-glutamine supplementation serves as a promising adjunct therapy to
reduce COVID-19 morbidity, particularly when administered early. However, its ultimate efficacy is highly
dependent on baseline metabolic status and disease timing. Future large-scale, baseline-stratified trials
are necessary to optimize administration protocols and mitigate potential viral replication risks.
*
1. Topic Landscape: L-Glutamine in Immune and Endothelial
Function
L-glutamine is the most abundant non-essential amino acid in the human body, constituting approximately
30% of the circulating plasma free amino acid pool [15]. During periods of extreme physiological stress,
catabolic states, or severe viral infections, endogenous production frequently fails to meet metabolic
demands, rendering L-glutamine a "conditionally essential" nutrient [2]. L-glutamine plays a critical role in
cellular homeostasis, acting as a primary oxidative fuel for rapidly dividing cells, including lymphocytes,
enterocytes, and macrophages [1]. Furthermore, adequate glutamine levels are vital for preserving
endothelial cellular integrity and facilitating robust, coordinated immune responses [3]. The attenuation of
inflammatory cascades and the maintenance of vascular health rely heavily on optimal glutamine
availability, highlighting its therapeutic potential in severe infectious diseases [1].
The pathogenesis of COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2, is heavily driven by aberrant systemic
inflammation and immune hyperactivation [9][12]. In severe clinical presentations, this hyperinflammatory
state frequently precipitates acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), systemic tissue hypoxemia, and
widespread endothelial damage [6][12], inducing profound metabolic dysregulation [13]. Advanced
metabolomic and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic profiling of COVID-19 patients
consistently reveals marked alterations in the blood serum metabolome, notably characterized by
significant deviations in plasma amino acid concentrations [4][7][19]. Chief among these metabolic
anomalies is a severe depletion of circulating glutamine and a marked dysregulation of the

---

glutamine/glutamate metabolic axis [8]. Because the catabolic rate during acute SARS-CoV-2 infection
drastically outpaces endogenous synthesis, the resulting glutamine deficiency actively promotes further
immune and endothelial cell dysfunction, exacerbating disease severity [3].
2. Key Questions and Sub-Questions Identified
The convergence of systemic glutamine depletion and severe COVID-19 symptomatology poses several
critical clinical questions regarding therapeutic intervention. Primarily, what is the direct impact of
exogenous L-glutamine supplementation on the morbidity and short-term mortality of patients admitted
with COVID-19?
Secondary sub-questions include: (i) Does the route of administration (enteral versus intravenous)
distinctly alter clinical outcomes such as ICU admission rates and hospitalization duration [1][15]? (ii) Can
L-glutamine supplementation effectively modulate and suppress the hyperinflammatory response
associated with SARS-CoV-2 [15]? (iii) How do we reconcile the immune-boosting benefits of glutamine
with recent evidence suggesting that coronaviruses may exploit host cellular glutamine metabolism to
facilitate their own viral replication [17]?
While early clinical trials suggest that L-glutamine may shorten hospital stays and reduce the need for
ICU-level care [1], its definitive role in preventing short-term mortality remains ambiguous and requires
deeper investigation [15].
Methods
Study Design and Guidelines Compliance To systematically address these research questions, this
exploratory investigation was structured in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic
reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines, an appropriate
framework for mapping emerging heterogeneous evidence [4][8]. By integrating clinical trial outcomes with
metabolomic profiling, this design allows for a comprehensive evaluation of how conditional amino acid
interventions alter the clinical trajectory of COVID-19 [2][11].
Ethical Considerations As this study constitutes a systematic scoping review and synthesis of previously
published and publicly available literature, institutional review board (IRB) ethical approval was waived.
Preliminary Literature Scan Methodology A comprehensive preliminary literature scan was conducted
to identify relevant clinical and metabolic studies. The search strategy utilized electronic databases,
primarily MEDLINE and the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, reflecting validated
methodologies for scoping reviews in clinical nutrition [2]. Search terms included combinations of
"COVID-19", "SARS-CoV-2", "L-glutamine", "glutamine supplementation", "metabolomics", "intensive
care", and "mortality".
Inclusion criteria for human studies required that articles report on adult patients diagnosed with COVID-19
who received oral or intravenous L-glutamine supplementation, or studies that analyzed
glutamine/glutamate metabolic dysregulation via NMR spectroscopy or quasi-targeted metabolomics
[4][8]. Exclusion criteria encompassed non-human in vivo models [18], purely in vitro analyses lacking
direct clinical translation [9], and studies where baseline nutritional status or supplementation parameters
were not quantifiable [2].
Risk of Bias Assessment To ensure data integrity, the risk of bias for included clinical trials was
assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2) for randomized trials and the Risk Of Bias In
Non-randomized Studies of Interventions (ROBINS-I) tool for observational cohorts. Studies exhibiting

---

critically high bias due to unmeasured confounding were noted during thematic synthesis.
Definition of Variables of Interest Primary and secondary variables of interest were explicitly defined:
(cid:127) L-glutamine Dosage and Route: The administration route (oral vs. intravenous) and dosage
protocols utilized during the acute phase of infection or ICU admission [1][15].
(cid:127) Hospitalization Time: Total duration (in days) from admission to discharge, utilized as a primary
metric for morbidity [1].
(cid:127) ICU Admission Rates: The proportion of patients requiring escalation to intensive care [1].
(cid:127) Mortality: Short-term mortality occurring within the observation period (e.g., 7-day ICU mortality or
total in-hospital mortality) [1][15].
Statistical and Thematic Analysis Plan Data synthesis employed a dual approach encompassing
thematic analysis of metabolomic data and statistical synthesis of clinical outcomes. Continuous variables
(e.g., hospitalization time) were analyzed by extracting mean durations, standard deviations, and reported
p-values to evaluate effect sizes [1]. Categorical variables (mortality, ICU admission rates) were evaluated
by comparing proportional differences between standard enteral nutrition (EN) cohorts and
glutamine-supplemented (GN) cohorts [15]. The statistical analysis plan ensured that all comparisons were
extracted symmetrically, with significance thresholds set at p < 0.05, and missing variables were explicitly
coded to prevent data truncation or skewed comparative weighting. Table 1 summarizes the planned
comparative data extraction framework.

Transitioning from our methodological framework, the synthesis of extracted data generated distinct
clinical and metabolomic themes regarding the efficacy of L-glutamine.
3. Preliminary Literature Scan with Emerging Themes
The synthesized literature reveals a focused pool of clinical data evaluating L-glutamine supplementation
in COVID-19 patients, primarily anchored by a prospective observational study of early-stage patients [1]
and a randomized clinical trial of severe ICU patients [15].
Impact on Morbidity: Hospitalization and ICU Requirements L-glutamine supplementation significantly
improves specific morbidity indicators when administered early. In a cohort of 60 early-stage patients,
those receiving oral L-glutamine exhibited a reduced mean hospitalization duration (8.9 ± 1.8 days)
compared to unsupplemented controls (10.4 ± 1.9 days) (p=0.005) [1]. Furthermore, oral supplementation
eliminated the need for ICU transfer in this cohort (0% vs. 13.3%, p=0.038) [1]. In critically ill populations,

---

intravenous glutamine added to enteral nutrition significantly reduced the rate of unsuccessful enteral
feeding; only 33.3% of supplemented patients required a transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN),
compared to 73.3% of controls (p=0.002) [15].
Mortality Outcomes Despite observable improvements in morbidity, evidence supporting L-glutamine's
efficacy in reducing mortality remains limited. In the ICU cohort, short-term (7-day) mortality was not
significantly altered by glutamine supplementation (33.3% vs. 37.6%) [15]. Similarly, in the earlier-stage
cohort, overall mortality was exceptionally low, preventing robust statistical conclusions regarding survival
[1].
4. Identified Knowledge Gaps
Despite promising signals regarding morbidity, fundamental gaps in the literature hinder the development
of standardized clinical guidelines. The scoping review by Rozga et al. highlights a critical deficiency: most
trials fail to measure baseline glutamine status prior to intervention, making it impossible to differentiate
between the correction of a deficiency and supra-therapeutic supplementation [2].
Key Knowledge Gaps include:
(cid:127) Optimal Timing and Dosing: Does early oral supplementation block viral progression, or does it
fuel viral replication [1][17]? Is late-stage IV intervention futile for mortality once hyperinflammation
has triggered irreversible tissue damage [15]?
(cid:127) Patient Stratification: How do patient-specific metabolic phenotypes—such as
hypertension-associated NMR metabolomic profiles [4] or cancer-induced immune cuproptosis
[14]—alter the efficacy of exogenous glutamine?
5. Potential Hypotheses for Further Investigation
Based on the exploratory synthesis and the identified gaps, the following testable hypotheses (speculative
insights) are proposed:
(cid:127) Hypothesis 1 (The Temporal Dependency Hypothesis): Early enteral L-glutamine
supplementation prevents progression to severe COVID-19 by preserving gut barrier integrity and
preventing dysbiosis, whereas late IV administration in the ICU primarily functions to mitigate systemic
hypercatabolism without altering viral-induced mortality.
(cid:127) Hypothesis 2 (The Phenotype Stratification Hypothesis): Patients with baseline endothelial
dysfunction (e.g., Sickle Cell Disease [16], Hypertension [4]) will exhibit a disproportionately greater
absolute risk reduction in COVID-19 morbidity when treated with L-glutamine compared to
normotensive, non-SCD patients.
(cid:127) Hypothesis 3 (The Viral Uncoupling Hypothesis): Viral hijacking of host glutamine metabolism
can be therapeutically uncoupled from host immune utilization through the co-administration of
targeted metabolic inhibitors alongside L-glutamine.
6. Suggested Methodologies for Each Hypothesis
To rigorously test these hypotheses, future investigations should employ the following methodologies:
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 1: Employ a longitudinal, multi-arm clinical cohort design administering L-glutamine
during early infection (viral replication phase) versus late infection (hyperinflammatory phase). Track
daily viral shedding (via RT-PCR) alongside continuous monitoring of inflammatory cytokines to
delineate the risk-benefit ratio at different disease stages [1][15].

---

(cid:127) For Hypothesis 2: Conduct prospective, multi-center, double-blind RCTs. Utilize quantitative NMR
spectroscopy and targeted metabolomics to establish baseline plasma glutamine/glutamate ratios
[4][8] before initiating supplementation. Stratify cohorts based on preexisting endothelial conditions
[4][16].
(cid:127) For Hypothesis 3: Perform in vitro metabolic tracing studies using stable isotope-labeled
L-glutamine in human airway epithelial cells. Pair glutamine supplementation with selective inhibitors
of viral nucleotide synthesis pathways, such as CTP synthetase 1 (CTPS1) inhibitors [5].
7. Cross-Disciplinary Connections
Integrating clinical findings with metabolomic profiling yields a profound cross-disciplinary landscape.
Quantitative NMR spectroscopy consistently identifies severe dysregulation of glutamine/glutamate
metabolism in COVID-19 patients, correlating strongly with clinical severity [4][8][11][19]. This provides a
mechanistic basis for clinical observations: L-glutamine replenishes depleted stores, dampening
inflammation [3].
However, a paradoxical speculative insight arises from virology: glutamine metabolism is fundamentally
essential for coronavirus replication within host cells [17]. This introduces a "metabolic tug-of-war."
Furthermore, insights from Oncology regarding immune cell cuproptosis and glutamine regulation [14] can
inform immunological endpoints in COVID-19 trials, while leveraging Hematology models of tissue
hypoxemia [6] can refine glutamine's use in mitigating COVID-19 vasculopathy, particularly in Sickle Cell
Disease [10][16].
8. Conclusion and Recommended Next Steps
This exploratory investigation highlights a complex but promising landscape regarding L-glutamine
administration in SARS-CoV-2 infection. Early evidence suggests L-glutamine serves as a beneficial
adjunct therapy, significantly shortening hospitalization time and reducing ICU admission in non-critical
patients [1], and mitigating the transition to total parenteral nutrition in critically ill patients [15]. However,
evidence supporting a direct reduction in short-term mortality remains inconclusive [1][15], complicated by
the viral reliance on host glutamine for replication [17].
To translate these exploratory findings into actionable clinical paradigms, the following next steps are
recommended, prioritized by urgency:
1. Priority 1 (Metabolomic-Stratified RCTs): Execute large-scale, double-blind RCTs that stratify
COVID-19 patients based on baseline plasma glutamine levels using NMR spectroscopy [11] to determine
if supplementation uniquely benefits those with proven deficiency [2].
2. Priority 2 (Viral Substrate Profiling): Initiate in vivo translational studies to map the competitive uptake
of labeled L-glutamine isotopes between host immune cells and SARS-CoV-2 replication machinery
[17][18].
3. Priority 3 (Special Population Protocols): Design targeted pilot studies for COVID-19 patients with
extra-pulmonary comorbidities, such as Sickle Cell Disease [6][10][16], leveraging existing L-glutamine
pharmacovigilance data to establish dual-benefit therapeutic protocols.
4. Priority 4 (Update Nutritional Guidelines): Policymakers should update interim guidelines to
recognize the potential of enteral L-glutamine in reducing hospital stay and TPN reliance [1][15], utilizing
digital health tools to monitor outpatient nutritional adherence during post-COVID recovery [13][20].
